fact: December 19 sits exactly 6 days before Christmas and 12 days before New Year's Eve - making it the perfect 'almost there but not quite' day!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls just 2-3 days before the winter solstice, meaning you're experiencing some of the shortest days of the entire year right now.
Reindeer in the Arctic are living in complete darkness around December 19 - the sun doesn't rise at all for weeks, so they navigate by moonlight and starlight!

Why do sunsets look extra orange and pink around this time? The sun sits so low in the sky that its light travels through way more atmosphere, filtering out blues and leaving behind those fiery colors.
December 19 falls smack in the middle of Sagittarius season, which means if you believe in zodiac signs, the universe is supposedly feeling extra adventurous and freedom-loving right now.
Penguins in Antarctica are dealing with their longest days ever around December 19 - while the north freezes in darkness, these tuxedo birds get nearly 24 hours of sunlight!
Tree sap moves so slowly in December that it's basically frozen syrup - if you tried to tap a maple tree now, you'd get maybe one sad drip per hour.
The moon around December 19 can look absolutely massive when it's low on the horizon because your brain compares it to buildings and trees, creating a wild optical illusion.
Bears in hibernation have been asleep for about a month by December 19, and their heart rate has dropped so low they're basically living in super slow motion.
December 19 marks roughly day 353 of the year, meaning you've survived about 97% of another trip around the sun - only 12 more days to go!
Evergreen trees like spruces and firs are showing off right now because they're the only plants still green and alive-looking while everything else looks dead and brown.
